Article.ArticleTitle: Synergistic design of bioretention cells for efficient inorganic nitrogen removal: Integrating submerged zone, media amendment, and porosity stratification via reactive transport modeling.

Abstract.AbstractText: Nitrogen in urban stormwater runoff dominantly exists in dissolved forms, exhibiting complex transport dynamics that challenge effective monitoring and limit pollution control efficiency. Bioretention cells (BRCs) have emerged as one of the primary stormwater management measures. However, conventional designs often rely on empirical approaches, with the coupled mechanisms governing transport, adsorption, and reaction for nitrogen removal remaining inadequately characterized. We employed controlled laboratory columnar experiments and multiphysics coupled numerical simulations to elucidate the fluid flow, advective-dispersive transport and biochemical reactions processes of inorganic nitrogen within BRCs. Columnar experiments demonstrated that incorporating a 300-mm submerged zone (SZ) improved nitrate removal from 69.3 % to 78.3 %, while iron-modified biochar (FeBC) amendment further enhanced this to 80 %. A reactive transport model capable of accurately simulating nitrogen removal was constructed, with R2 values exceeding 0.5. Sensitivity analysis revealed that media adsorption capacity, nitrification/denitrification rates, and media porosity were identified as the key design parameters governing removal efficiency. Consequently, a functional gradient optimization strategy was proposed, with the most efficient stratified porosity design, where the upper layer media was composed of sand, vermiculite, zeolite, volcanic rock, and FeBC with a porosity of 0.45-0.55 and the lower layer media was composed of fine sand, FeBC and activated sludge with a porosity of 0.35-0.45, achieved synergistic removal of both NH4+-N (>96 %) and NO3--N (>81 %). Compared to conventional single-layer systems, the proposed model improves N removal by more than 11 %, addressing the long-standing challenge of low N removal efficiency and proposing a stratified media optimization framework for practical implementation of BRCs.
